at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: File in the Appellate Division of Superior Court

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If your claim exceeds $5,000, you must either waive the excess amount to file in Small Claims or file in the Special Civil Part for claims up to $20,000. Splitting one claim into multiple cases to stay within the limit is not permitted.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: New Jersey allows you to file claims for breaches of written or oral contracts, personal loans, and unpaid invoices in Small Claims Court, provided the amount does not exceed $5,000.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: Ensure you bring the signed contract, proof of payment, and any correspondence related to the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally with a landscaper to pay $1,500 for yard work, paid upfront, and they never showed up.
claims.notes: Gather any evidence of the agreement, such as emails, text messages, or witness statements.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You lent a friend $2,000 with the promise of repayment in six months, but they haven't paid you back.
claims.notes: Provide documentation of the loan agreement and any attempts to collect the debt.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: A borrower signed a promissory note to repay $3,500 over a year but stopped making payments after two months.
claims.notes: Bring the original promissory note and records of payments made and missed.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided graphic design services worth $1,200 to a client who has not paid the invoice.
claims.notes: Submit the invoice, proof of service delivery, and any communication regarding payment.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $4,500 on their account with your business and refuses to pay.
claims.notes: Provide account statements, terms of the account agreement, and records of payment history.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $3,000 in back rent and has vacated the property without paying.
claims.notes: Bring the lease agreement, records of rent payments, and any notices sent to the tenant.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid for goods with a $500 check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: Present the bounced check, bank notice of insufficient funds, and any related correspondence.

claims.name: Co-signer held liable for default
claims.example: You co-signed a $4,000 loan for a friend who defaulted, and now the lender is seeking payment from you.
claims.notes: Provide the loan agreement, proof of default, and any demands for payment you've received.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: New Jersey's Small Claims Court handles various consumer disputes, including defective products and unsatisfactory services, with a claim limit of $5,000.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,200 refrigerator that stopped working after two months, and the seller refuses to repair or replace it.
claims.notes: Bring the purchase receipt, warranty information, and any communication with the seller.

claims.name: Unsatisfactory service
claims.example: A cleaning service charged you $800 but did not perform the agreed-upon tasks.
claims.notes: Provide the service agreement, proof of payment, and photos or descriptions of the incomplete work.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $1,500 for repairs that did not fix the issue, and now refuses to refund or re-repair.
claims.notes: Submit the repair invoice, any warranties, and evidence of the ongoing problem.

claims.name: Contractor failed to complete work
claims.example: You paid a contractor $4,500 to renovate your bathroom, but they abandoned the project halfway through.
claims.notes: Bring the contract, proof of payments, and photos of the unfinished work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver goods
claims.example: You ordered $2,000 worth of furniture that was never delivered, and the vendor is unresponsive.
claims.notes: Provide the order confirmation, payment proof, and any correspondence with the vendor.

claims.name: Unauthorized subscription charges
claims.example: A gym continued to charge your account $100 monthly despite your cancellation request.
claims.notes: Submit the cancellation notice, bank statements showing charges, and any communication with the gym.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $4,800 that broke down within a week, and the dealer refuses to address the issue.
claims.notes: Bring the sales contract, any warranties, and repair estimates.

claims.name: False advertising
claims.example: A retailer advertised a laptop for $900 with specific features, but the delivered product lacked those features.
claims.notes: Provide the advertisement, purchase receipt, and evidence of the product's deficiencies.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Claims for property damage, such as vehicle collisions or damage to rental property, can be filed in New Jersey's Small Claims Court if the amount is $5,000 or less.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver hit your parked car, causing $3,500 in damages, and refuses to pay for repairs.
claims.notes: Bring the police report, repair estimates, and any communication with the other driver.

claims.name: Neighbor caused property damage
claims.example: Your neighbor's tree fell onto your fence during a storm, causing $2,000 in damages.
claims.notes: Provide photos of the damage, repair estimates, and any correspondence with the neighbor.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A former tenant left your rental unit with $4,500 in damages beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: Bring the lease agreement, move-in and move-out inspection reports, and repair invoices.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog entered your yard and destroyed $1,200 worth of landscaping.
claims.notes: Provide photos of the damage, repair estimates, and any communication with the pet owner.

claims.name: Damage to property in someone's custody
claims.example: You left your watch for repair at a jeweler, and they returned it with scratches and a cracked face.
claims.notes: Bring the repair receipt, photos of the damage, and any correspondence with the jeweler.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes (money only)
claim_categories.blurb: Monetary disputes between landlords and tenants, such as security deposit issues or unpaid rent, are handled in New Jersey's Small Claims Court for amounts up to $5,000.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,500 security deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: Provide the lease agreement, move-out inspection report, and any correspondence with the landlord.

claims.name: Security deposit deductions not itemized
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $800 from your security deposit without specifying the reasons.
claims.notes: Bring the lease agreement, any communication regarding the deductions, and evidence of the property's condition.

claims.name: Uninhabitable rental conditions
claims.example: Your rental unit had severe mold issues, and the landlord refused to address them, forcing you to move out.
claims.notes: Provide photos of the conditions, repair requests, and any health department reports.

claims.name: Landlord retained tenant's property
claims.example: After eviction, your landlord kept your furniture and personal belongings worth $3,000.
claims.notes: List the items taken, their estimated value, and any communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your apartment.
claims.notes: Document the eviction, any notices received, and communication with the landlord.

claims.name: Failure to return prepaid rent
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent, but moved out early, and the landlord refused to refund the unused portion.
claims.notes: Provide the lease agreement, proof of payment, and any correspondence regarding the refund.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant missed two months' rent totaling $2,800 and has since vacated the property.
claims.notes: Bring the lease agreement, payment records, and any notices sent to the tenant.

claims.name: Tenant deducted repair costs from rent
claims.example: You repaired a broken heater costing $600 and deducted it from your rent, but the landlord is demanding full payment.
claims.notes: Provide repair receipts, communication with the landlord, and evidence of the issue.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Employees can file claims in New Jersey's Small Claims Court for unpaid wages, final paychecks, and other employment-related monetary disputes up to $5,000.

claims.name: Unpaid wages
claims.example: Your employer failed to pay you for 40 hours of work, totaling $800.
claims.notes: Bring pay stubs, timesheets, and any communication with your employer regarding the unpaid wages.

claims.name: Withheld final paycheck
claims.example: After resigning, your employer refused to issue your final paycheck of $1,200.
claims.notes: Provide resignation notice, pay stubs, and any correspondence with the employer.

claims.name: Unpaid commissions
claims.example: You earned $3,500 in sales commissions that your employer has not paid.
claims.notes: Submit the commission agreement, sales records, and payment history.

claims.name: Unpaid overtime
claims.example: Your employer did not pay overtime rates for 20 extra hours worked, totaling $600.
claims.notes: Bring timesheets, pay stubs, and any communication regarding overtime policies.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Individuals can seek compensation in New Jersey's Small Claims Court for minor injuries, loaned property, and civil theft, provided the claim does not exceed $5,000.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on a wet floor in a store, resulting in a sprained ankle and $1,500 in medical bills.
claims.notes: Provide medical records, photos of the hazard, and any incident reports.

claims.name: Unreturned loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your $800 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: Document the loan agreement, any requests for return, and the item's value.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: A roommate took your $1,200 laptop without permission and sold it.
claims.notes: Provide proof of ownership, police reports, and any communication with the roommate.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Disputes related to vehicles, such as mechanic issues or wrongful towing, can be addressed in New Jersey's Small Claims Court for amounts up to $5,000.

claims.name: Dispute over vehicle repairs
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $2,000 for repairs that did not fix the problem, and refuses to refund or re-repair.
claims.notes: Bring the repair invoice, any warranties, and evidence of the ongoing issue.

claims.name: Wrongful towing of vehicle
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and the towing company demands $300 for its release.
claims.notes: Provide photos of the parking area, receipts, and any communication with the towing company.

exclusions.explanation: Eviction cases are handled in the Landlord-Tenant section of the Special Civil Part, not in Small Claims Court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: File in the Landlord-Tenant section of the Special Civil Part.

exclusions.explanation: Disputes involving ownership or title to real estate exceed the jurisdiction of Small Claims Court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: File in the Civil Part of the Law Division of the Superior Court.

exclusions.explanation: Claims for defamation, including libel and slander, are not suitable for Small Claims Court due to their complexity.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: Consult an attorney to file in the appropriate division of the Superior Court.

exclusions.explanation: Professional malpractice claims require expert testimony and are beyond the scope of Small Claims Court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: File in the Civil Part of the Law Division of the Superior Court.

exclusions.explanation: Issues such as divorce, child custody, and support are handled by the Family Part of the Superior Court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: File in the Family Part of the Superior Court.

exclusions.explanation: Disputes over wills, estates, and trusts are under the jurisdiction of the Surrogate's Court.
Small Claims Court does not have jurisdiction over cases against federal entities.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: File in the appropriate federal court.

exclusions.explanation: Class action lawsuits are complex and exceed the procedural capacity of Small Claims Court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: File in the Civil Part of the Law Division of the Superior Court.

exclusions.explanation: Small Claims Court cannot issue orders requiring a party to perform or refrain from specific acts.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: File in the Civil Part of the Law Division of the Superior Court.

exclusions.explanation: These claims are handled by the Division of Workers' Compensation, not Small Claims Court.
exclusions.where_to_go_instead: File with the Division of Workers' Compensation.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not awarded in Small Claims Court; the focus is on compensatory damages.
damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees may be recoverable in certain cases, such as those involving bad checks or consumer fraud, as specified by statute.

venue_rules.scenario: Defendant resides or does business in New Jersey
filing_options: County where the defendant lives or has a business
County where any one of the defendants lives or has a business
County where the cause of the complaint occurred

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: Complaints can be filed online through the Judiciary Electronic Document Submission (JEDS) system.

service_fees.notes: Service by certified and regular mail is currently $10 per defendant.
Personal service by a Court Officer is $10 per defendant.
For each additional defendant served personally by a Court Officer, the fee is $12.

eligibility_bullets: Income at or below 150% of the current poverty level
No more than $2,500 in liquid assets
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing and copy fees; service fees may also be waived at the judge's discretion.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win your case, the waived fees are typically added to the judgment against the defendant.

other_fees.name: Appeal filing
other_fees.notes: Fee for filing a Notice of Appeal in the Appellate Division.

other_fees.name: Transcript request
other_fees.notes: Cost depends on the length of the transcript and delivery time.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing and service fees are typically added to the judgment against the defendant.

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule may apply to certain claims, such as fraud, where the statute of limitations begins when the injury is discovered or reasonably should have been discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: Statutes of limitations may be tolled under certain circumstances, such as when the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or during bankruptcy proceedings.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can demonstrate good faith and may lead to a resolution without court intervention.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: For bad check claims, it's advisable to send a written demand for payment before filing a lawsuit.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: Employees should formally request unpaid wages from their employer before initiating legal action.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide tenants with a written itemization of damages and return any remaining security deposit within 30 days of lease termination.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: Consumers alleging fraud should document all interactions and may need to file a complaint with the Division of Consumer Affairs before pursuing legal action.

forms.name: Small Claims Complaint Form
forms.description: Initiates a small claims lawsuit by outlining the plaintiff's allegations and the relief sought.

forms.name: Fee Waiver Request
forms.description: Requests a waiver of court fees for individuals who cannot afford them.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal
forms.description: Notifies the court and opposing party of the intent to appeal a small claims decision.

methods.name: Certified and regular mail
methods.description: The court sends the complaint to the defendant via certified and regular mail.
cons: May be less reliable if defendant does not accept mail

methods.name: Personal service by Court Officer
methods.description: A Court Officer personally delivers the complaint to the defendant.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent or an officer of the corporation; for sole proprietors, serve the individual directly.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with New Jersey's long-arm statute and may require additional steps.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, service by publication may be permitted with court approval.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If a defendant is evading service, the court may allow alternative methods upon demonstration of due diligence.

response_and_default.response_notes: Defendants are not required to file a written answer but should appear in court on the scheduled date.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in favor of the plaintiff.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim before a judgment is entered.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Parties may be represented by attorneys but are not required to do so.
hearing.format: Bench trial with each party presenting their case; hearings are typically brief.
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter in advance through the court clerk's office.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within a few weeks.
hearing.on_site_mediation_notes: Some counties offer mediation services on the day of the hearing.

appeals.type_notes: The appellate court reviews the record from the small claims hearing; no new evidence is presented.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment can be appealed, but it is advisable to first file a motion to vacate the judgment in the trial court.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Obtain a portion of the debtor's wages until the judgment is satisfied.
methods.description: File a writ of execution with the court, serve it to the debtor's employer, who will then withhold a portion of the debtor's wages and remit it to the court.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor is employed and earns above the exemption threshold.
exemptions: 75% of disposable earnings or 30 times the federal minimum wage, whichever is greater

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from the debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, serve it to the debtor's bank, which will freeze the account and remit funds up to the judgment amount.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor has sufficient funds in the account; timing is crucial to prevent withdrawals.
exemptions: $1,000 in the bank account is exempt from levy

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on the debtor's real property, preventing its sale or refinancing until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a judgment lien with the county clerk where the debtor owns property; the lien must be satisfied before the property can be sold or refinanced.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective for securing payment but may take time until the debtor decides to sell or refinance the property.
exemptions: Homestead exemption may apply, protecting a portion of the property's value

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Authorize a court officer to seize and sell the debtor's non-exempt property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, which directs a court officer to seize the debtor's property, auction it, and apply the proceeds to the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if the debtor owns valuable non-exempt property; process can be time-consuming and may not yield full judgment amount.
exemptions: Certain personal property and tools of the trade are exempt from seizure

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before the 20-year period expires; renewal extends the judgment for an additional 20 years.
exempt_assets: Social Security and other federal benefits
collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy imposes an automatic stay on collection efforts; certain judgments may be discharged depending on the bankruptcy chapter filed.
collection.priority_notes: Judgment liens are prioritized based on the date of recording; earlier liens have higher priority over later ones.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses can represent themselves in Small Claims Court without an attorney.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a trade name must file a Trade Name Certificate with the county clerk.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: Sole proprietors may authorize an employee to represent them in court.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may be barred from suing to recover fees for work requiring a license.

pitfalls.title: Incorrect defendant information
pitfalls.what_happens: If you sue the wrong party or provide incorrect information, your case may be dismissed or you may obtain an unenforceable judgment.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Verify the exact legal name and address of the defendant before filing your complaint.

pitfalls.title: Exceeding the monetary limit
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing a claim exceeding $5,000 in Small Claims Court can result in dismissal or the need to waive the excess amount.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Ensure your claim does not exceed the $5,000 limit or consider filing in the Special Civil Part for larger amounts.

pitfalls.title: Missing the statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing after the statute of limitations has expired will likely result in dismissal of your case.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Determine the applicable statute of limitations for your claim type and file within that period.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant is not properly served, the court may not have jurisdiction, leading to delays or dismissal.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow the court's rules for service of process carefully, using certified mail or a court officer as appropriate.

pitfalls.title: Lack of evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Insufficient evidence can lead to losing your case, even if your claim is valid.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, photos, and witness statements to support your claim.

pitfalls.title: Ignoring court deadlines
pitfalls.what_happens: Missing filing deadlines or court dates can result in dismissal or default judgments against you.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Keep track of all deadlines and court dates, and respond promptly to court notices.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, you are not required to have a lawyer in Small Claims Court. The process is designed to be accessible to individuals representing themselves.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: The timeline varies, but cases are typically resolved within 30 to 90 days from filing to judgment.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: The maximum amount you can sue for in Small Claims Court is $5,000.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear, the court may enter a default judgment in your favor, provided you present sufficient evidence to support your claim.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a Small Claims Court decision by filing a Notice of Appeal within 45 days of the judgment.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment is valid for 20 years and can be renewed for an additional 20 years if necessary.

state_specific_notes: New Jersey allows businesses to represent themselves in Small Claims Court without an attorney.
The state provides free interpreter services for individuals who require language assistance during court proceedings.
Small Claims Court in New Jersey does not handle eviction cases; these are addressed in the Landlord-Tenant section of the Special Civil Part.
Filing fees in New Jersey's Small Claims Court are relatively low, with a $35 fee for one defendant and $5 for each additional defendant.
New Jersey's Small Claims Court has a monetary limit of $5,000 for claims, which is lower than some other states.

interesting_findings: New Jersey's Small Claims Court offers an online filing system through the Judiciary Electronic Document Submission (JEDS) platform, enhancing accessibility for plaintiffs.
The state provides detailed self-help resources and forms on its judiciary website, assisting individuals in navigating the Small Claims process without legal representation.

sources.label: NJ Courts — Special Civil Part (small claims)
N.J. Stat. § 2A:14-1 et seq. (Statute of limitations)
NJ Rules of Court Part VI (Special Civil Part)
NJ Special Civil Part filing fees
NJ Rule 6:2 (Service of process — Special Civil Part)
N.J. Stat. § 2A:17-50 et seq. (Execution / wage execution)
N.J. Stat. § 56:8-1 et seq. (Consumer Fraud Act)
